title: Affordable Housing Policy and Neighborhood Stabilization in Post-Industrial Cities
description: This study examines the effectiveness of affordable housing policies in stabilizing neighborhoods undergoing economic decline in post-industrial urban areas. Using panel data from twelve northeastern cities, we find that inclusionary zoning combined with community land trusts significantly reduces displacement of low-income residents.

title: Gentrification Displacement and Community Land Trusts: A Rhode Island Case Study
description: This paper investigates how community land trusts function as anti-displacement tools in Rhode Island neighborhoods experiencing rapid gentrification. Findings show that CLT properties maintained affordability across market cycles while preserving resident tenure and neighborhood social cohesion.

title: Rent Burden and Housing Insecurity Among Low-Income Renters in New England
description: This study analyzes rent burden trends among low-income renters across New England using American Community Survey data from 2010 to 2022. Results indicate that housing cost-burdened households face significantly higher rates of food insecurity, healthcare avoidance, and residential mobility.

title: Income Volatility and Asset-Building Among Working Families
description: This research examines how income volatility affects the capacity of working-class families to accumulate financial assets and build long-term economic stability. Using longitudinal survey data, we find that households with high earnings variability are significantly less likely to maintain emergency savings or contribute to retirement accounts.

title: Racial Wealth Gap Trends in the United States 2000-2020
description: This paper tracks the evolution of the racial wealth gap between Black, Hispanic, and white households over two decades using Survey of Consumer Finances data. We document persistent and widening disparities in net worth attributable to differential homeownership rates, inheritance, and access to financial markets.

title: Social Network Analysis of Political Polarization on Social Media
description: This study applies network analysis techniques to large-scale social media data to examine structural patterns of political polarization across ideological communities. Results reveal increasing cross-partisan link scarcity and the emergence of echo chambers that limit exposure to opposing viewpoints.

title: Large-Scale Analysis of Online Misinformation Propagation
description: This research analyzes the spread dynamics of health and political misinformation across social media platforms using a dataset of over 50 million posts. We identify structural features of early-adopter networks and platform affordances that predict viral misinformation cascades.

title: Machine Learning Approaches to Survey Non-Response Bias Correction
description: This paper evaluates multiple machine learning algorithms for correcting non-response bias in large-scale social surveys. Gradient boosting and neural network post-stratification approaches outperform traditional raking and propensity weighting methods, especially for subgroup estimation.

title: Topic Modeling Nineteenth Century American Periodicals
description: This study applies latent Dirichlet allocation and neural topic models to a corpus of 19th-century American periodical literature to reveal evolving cultural and political discourse patterns. Results document how themes of industrialization, immigration, and racial politics shifted across decades in the popular press.

title: Digital Archive and Visualization of Indigenous Land Records
description: This project develops a digital archive and geospatial visualization platform for historical indigenous land records held across multiple New England repositories. The system enables researchers and tribal communities to access, annotate, and contextualize colonial-era land conveyance documents.

title: Zoning Reform and Housing Supply: Evidence from Rhode Island Municipalities
description: This study examines the effects of zoning liberalization on housing construction in Rhode Island municipalities using a difference-in-differences design. We find that upzoning near transit corridors and town centers produces measurable increases in multifamily building permits within three years of reform.

title: Transit-Oriented Development and Equity in Small Metro Regions
description: This paper investigates whether transit-oriented development in smaller metropolitan areas produces equitable access to affordable housing, employment, and services for low-income residents. Using case studies from four New England metro areas, we identify design and policy conditions that promote inclusive TOD outcomes.

title: Municipal Fiscal Stress and Service Delivery in Declining Cities
description: This research examines how fiscal stress affects the delivery of core municipal services in cities experiencing population and economic decline. Analysis of budget and service-level data from 40 northeastern cities finds that fiscal distress disproportionately reduces spending on public health and maintenance rather than administrative functions.

title: Economic Shock Adaptation in Deindustrialized Coastal Communities
description: This study examines how coastal communities in the northeastern United States adapted economically following the collapse of manufacturing and commercial fishing industries. We identify diversification into tourism, small-scale entrepreneurship, and institutional anchoring as key resilience strategies.

title: Social Capital and Disaster Recovery: Lessons from Recent Hurricanes
description: This paper investigates the role of bridging and bonding social capital in the speed and equity of community recovery following recent hurricane disasters. Communities with high pre-disaster social capital and active civic organizations recovered critical infrastructure and housing stocks significantly faster.

title: Hybrid Theatre: Audience Engagement in Mixed-Reality Performance
description: This study explores how hybrid theatre productions integrating live performance with real-time mixed-reality environments affect audience engagement and emotional response. Through experimental production and audience surveys, we identify design principles that deepen empathy and presence in co-located and remote participants.

title: Participatory Storytelling and Community Voice in Regional Theater
description: This research examines the use of participatory storytelling methodologies in regional theater to amplify marginalized community voices and foster civic dialogue. Case studies from three New England companies demonstrate that devised community-centered productions increase both audience diversity and civic participation.

title: Oral History Methodology and the Preservation of Immigrant Narratives
description: This paper examines methodological challenges and ethical considerations in collecting oral histories from immigrant communities, with attention to language, power, and informed consent. We propose a community-centered framework that prioritizes narrator agency and long-term archive stewardship.

title: Public Art Memory and Contested Histories in Urban Space
description: This research investigates how public art installations in urban spaces engage with contested historical memories and produce new forms of civic meaning-making. Through qualitative analysis of monument debates and participatory art projects, we identify conditions under which public art fosters rather than forecloses historical dialogue.

title: Digitizing the Past: Archival Access and Cultural Equity
description: This study examines how digitization projects in cultural heritage institutions shape access to historical records and who benefits from expanded online availability. We find persistent disparities in digitization investment that replicate rather than redress historical patterns of archival exclusion.

title: Attention Mechanisms in Medical Image Segmentation
description: This paper introduces a novel attention-guided convolutional neural network architecture for semantic segmentation of anatomical structures in medical imaging data. The proposed model achieves state-of-the-art Dice scores on multi-organ segmentation benchmarks while requiring substantially less labeled training data.

title: Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Clinical Prediction Models
description: This study develops and evaluates a federated learning framework for training clinical risk prediction models across multiple hospital systems without sharing patient data. The federated approach achieves predictive performance comparable to centralized training while providing formal differential privacy guarantees.

title: Reward Shaping Strategies in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
description: This paper investigates the effect of reward shaping on convergence speed and policy quality in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning environments. We demonstrate that potential-based shaping with shared value function baselines significantly reduces sample complexity in multi-agent coordination tasks.

title: Differential Privacy in Distributed Machine Learning Systems
description: This research analyzes the privacy-utility tradeoffs of differential privacy mechanisms applied to gradient aggregation in distributed machine learning pipelines. We propose an adaptive noise calibration scheme that maintains model accuracy while providing strong provable privacy guarantees across heterogeneous data silos.

title: Intrusion Detection Using Anomaly-Based Graph Neural Networks
description: This paper presents a graph neural network approach to network intrusion detection that models host-level communication patterns as dynamic graphs and flags anomalous subgraph structures. The system achieves high detection rates for novel attack variants while significantly reducing false positive alerts compared to signature-based approaches.

title: Tuition Discount Rates and Net Revenue Sustainability in Private Colleges
description: This study analyzes the relationship between institutional tuition discounting practices and long-run net revenue sustainability in private four-year colleges. Using financial data spanning two decades, we identify discount rate thresholds beyond which institutions face systemic risk of enrollment-driven revenue shortfalls.

title: First-Generation Student Completion: Institutional Practices and Outcomes
description: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of institutional support programs for first-generation college students on six-year completion rates at a diverse sample of four-year institutions. Findings identify proactive advising, financial aid simplification, and peer mentoring as the highest-impact interventions.

title: Federal Financial Aid Reform and Access to Selective Institutions
description: This research examines how proposed reforms to federal need-based financial aid would affect enrollment of low-income students at selective colleges and universities. Simulation analysis suggests that increasing maximum Pell Grant awards and simplifying FAFSA substantially improves access for the lowest-income applicants.

title: Predictive Enrollment Modeling Using Integrated CRM and SIS Data
description: This paper develops a predictive model for undergraduate enrollment yield using integrated customer relationship management and student information system data. Machine learning classifiers trained on communication engagement, campus visit behavior, and academic preparation outperform traditional statistical models for predicting matriculation.

title: Early Alert Systems and Academic Intervention Efficacy
description: This study evaluates the impact of early alert systems on academic outcomes for at-risk undergraduate students across multiple intervention types. Analysis of administrative data shows that advisor outreach within 48 hours of an alert flag increases course completion rates and reduces stop-out by measurable margins.

title: Student Venture Survival Rates and Mentorship Outcomes
description: This paper investigates the relationship between entrepreneurial mentorship intensity and survival rates of student-founded ventures in university incubator programs. We find that structured mentorship with experienced entrepreneurs significantly increases three-year venture survival and revenue growth compared to peer-only support models.

title: Social Impact Measurement Frameworks for Campus Startups
description: This research evaluates competing frameworks for measuring the social impact of ventures launched through university entrepreneurship programs. We propose an adapted theory-of-change approach that integrates stakeholder-defined outcomes with quantitative performance indicators relevant to campus-based social enterprises.

title: University Technology Transfer and Spin-Off Creation in Small States
description: This study examines the determinants of successful university technology transfer and spin-off firm formation in small-state contexts where regional venture capital and industry density are limited. We identify licensing strategy, faculty entrepreneurial intent, and industry partnership strength as primary predictors of commercialization success.

title: Default Effects and Retirement Savings Enrollment in Workplace Plans
description: This paper studies the behavioral effects of automatic enrollment defaults on retirement savings participation and contribution rates in workplace defined-contribution plans. We find that opt-out default designs increase participation by over 30 percentage points, with especially large effects among younger and lower-income workers.

title: Dynamic Pricing Fairness Perceptions Among E-Commerce Consumers
description: This research investigates how consumers perceive algorithmic dynamic pricing practices in e-commerce environments and the factors that mediate fairness judgments. Experimental and survey findings indicate that transparency about pricing logic and purchase history usage strongly moderate consumer trust and purchase intentions.

title: Algorithmic Accountability and Due Process in Automated Decision Systems
description: This paper examines the legal and ethical frameworks governing automated decision-making systems used in public administration, criminal justice, and social services. We argue that existing due process protections are inadequate for algorithmic contexts and propose a set of explainability and contestation standards.

title: Surveillance Capitalism and the Limits of Consent-Based Data Governance
description: This research critically examines whether consent-based data governance regimes can meaningfully constrain surveillance capitalist data extraction and behavioral profiling practices. We argue that structural power asymmetries render informed consent largely fictional and propose alternative regulatory frameworks grounded in fiduciary duty.

title: AI Ethics Frameworks: A Comparative Analysis of Corporate and Regulatory Approaches
description: This paper systematically compares AI ethics frameworks published by major technology corporations with regulatory proposals from the EU, US, and international bodies across dimensions including accountability, transparency, and harm prevention. We identify persistent gaps between voluntary corporate commitments and binding regulatory requirements.

title: Epistemic Criteria for Scientific Consensus: Climate Science as a Case Study
description: This paper examines the philosophical criteria by which scientific communities establish and communicate consensus positions, using climate science as an extended case study. We analyze how expert disagreement at the research frontier is misappropriated in public discourse to manufacture uncertainty about settled findings.

title: Reproducibility Crisis and the Sociology of Scientific Knowledge
description: This research applies sociological analysis to the ongoing reproducibility crisis in social and biomedical sciences, examining how incentive structures, publication norms, and methodological communities shape replication failure rates. We argue that open science practices must be coupled with structural reforms in academic career evaluation.

title: Multi-Robot Task Allocation for Search and Rescue in Unstructured Environments
description: This paper presents a decentralized task allocation algorithm for heterogeneous robot teams deployed in search and rescue scenarios within structurally compromised indoor environments. The approach combines auction-based coordination with real-time mapping to optimize survivor detection coverage under communication uncertainty.

title: Sim-to-Real Transfer of Learned Locomotion Policies for Quadrupedal Robots
description: This study investigates techniques for transferring reinforcement learning locomotion policies trained in physics simulation to real quadrupedal robot hardware. Domain randomization over physical parameters combined with an adaptive residual policy layer achieves robust sim-to-real transfer across varied terrain conditions.

title: Drone Swarm Coordination Under Communication Constraints
description: This paper addresses the problem of coordinating large drone swarms for area coverage tasks when inter-agent communication bandwidth is severely constrained. We develop a distributed consensus protocol based on intermittent gossip communication that achieves near-optimal coverage with minimal message passing.

title: Shape Memory Polymer Composites for Soft Robotic Actuators
description: This research develops and characterizes novel shape memory polymer composite materials for use as thermally actuated soft robotic end-effectors. The composites exhibit high actuation force-to-weight ratios and programmable shape recovery profiles suitable for adaptive grasping applications.

title: Piezoelectric Nanogenerators for Self-Powered Wearable Sensors
description: This paper presents the fabrication and characterization of flexible piezoelectric nanogenerators capable of harvesting mechanical energy from human body motion to power wearable biosensors. The devices achieve sufficient power output for continuous wireless transmission of physiological signals under normal activity conditions.

title: Seismic Fragility Assessment of Reinforced Concrete Bridges in the Northeast
description: This study develops probabilistic seismic fragility curves for reinforced concrete highway bridges in the northeastern United States using incremental dynamic analysis with regionally calibrated ground motion records. Results indicate that a substantial proportion of the bridge inventory exceeds code-intended damage thresholds under moderate seismic scenarios.

title: Post-Hurricane Structural Damage Reconnaissance and Failure Analysis
description: This paper reports findings from rapid post-hurricane reconnaissance of structural damage patterns in coastal residential and commercial construction, with emphasis on roof-to-wall connection failures and storm surge loading effects. A systematic failure taxonomy is developed to inform building code improvements for hurricane-prone coastal zones.

title: Sensor-Based Structural Health Monitoring for Aging Bridge Infrastructure
description: This research presents a wireless sensor network deployment and data analysis framework for continuous structural health monitoring of aging steel and concrete highway bridges. Machine learning anomaly detection applied to acceleration and strain data successfully identified early-stage fatigue cracking in field deployment trials.

title: Life-Cycle Carbon Assessment of Low-Carbon Concrete Alternatives
description: This study conducts a comprehensive life-cycle carbon assessment comparing conventional Portland cement concrete with supplementary cementitious material blends including fly ash, slag, and calcined clay. Results demonstrate carbon reduction potential of 30 to 50 percent with optimized blended cement formulations without compromising structural performance.

title: Circular Economy Pathways for End-of-Life Infrastructure Materials
description: This paper investigates technical and economic pathways for integrating end-of-life infrastructure materials into circular economy supply chains, with a focus on reclaimed concrete aggregate and structural steel. Scenario analysis shows that closed-loop material flows are economically viable at metropolitan scale with appropriate policy incentives.

title: Rapid Iteration Design Methodology for Student Capstone Engineering Projects
description: This study evaluates a structured rapid iteration design methodology applied to senior capstone engineering projects, measuring effects on design quality, team performance, and learning outcomes. The methodology integrates weekly prototype testing cycles with user feedback and significantly improved final design scores and student self-reported engineering competency.

title: 3D-Printed Lattice Structures for Lightweight Load-Bearing Components
description: This paper characterizes the mechanical performance of additively manufactured polymer and metal lattice structures optimized for load-bearing applications through topology optimization. Experimental testing validates finite element predictions and demonstrates specific stiffness values competitive with traditional subtractive manufacturing for complex geometries.

title: Design-Build-Test Cycles in Interdisciplinary Engineering Education
description: This research examines how structured design-build-test project cycles in interdisciplinary engineering courses affect student learning, teamwork competencies, and professional identity formation. Longitudinal assessment data from a three-year cohort study show significant improvements in systems thinking and cross-disciplinary communication skills.

title: Thermal Management Strategies for High-Power Density Electric Vehicle Batteries
description: This paper investigates active and passive thermal management strategies for maintaining lithium-ion battery pack temperature uniformity in high-power density electric vehicle applications. Comparative analysis of liquid cooling, phase change material, and hybrid approaches reveals significant performance differences under dynamic drive cycle loading.

title: Turbulent Heat Transfer Enhancement in Microchannels for Electronics Cooling
description: This study experimentally and numerically investigates turbulence-induced heat transfer enhancement in microchannel heat sinks for high-heat-flux electronics cooling applications. Microfeature patterning on channel walls achieves significant Nusselt number improvements with acceptable pressure drop penalties compared to smooth-wall baselines.

title: Place-Based Environmental Education and Ecological Identity in Adolescents
description: This research examines how place-based environmental education programs affect ecological identity development and pro-environmental behavior intentions among middle and high school students in coastal communities. Students participating in immersive field-based curricula showed significantly stronger place attachment and environmental agency than control groups.

title: Citizen Science and Science Self-Efficacy in Middle School Students
description: This paper evaluates the effects of participation in structured citizen science projects on science self-efficacy, interest, and identity among middle school students from underrepresented groups. Results indicate meaningful gains in science confidence and career interest, particularly for female and first-generation college-aspiring students.

title: Curriculum Development for Climate Literacy in K-12 Coastal Communities
description: This study describes the development, implementation, and evaluation of a K-12 climate literacy curriculum tailored to the lived coastal environments of students in southern New England. Pre-post assessment data demonstrate significant gains in climate systems knowledge and local risk perception across grade levels.

title: Long-Term Monitoring of Biodiversity in Southern New England Forests
description: This paper reports twenty years of biodiversity monitoring data from permanent forest plots in southern New England, tracking changes in tree species composition, understory diversity, and wildlife community structure. Significant shifts in species assemblages are documented and attributed to climate warming, invasive species pressure, and land use change.

title: Invasive Plant Species Distribution and Native Community Displacement
description: This research maps the distribution and expansion trajectories of six major invasive plant species across southern New England using remote sensing and field survey data, assessing impacts on native plant community composition and richness. Results show that invasion hotspots are strongly associated with disturbed edge habitats and road corridors.

title: Optimal Sizing of Solar-Plus-Storage Systems for Campus Microgrids
description: This paper presents an optimization framework for sizing solar photovoltaic and battery storage systems in campus microgrid configurations to minimize lifecycle costs while meeting reliability and carbon reduction targets. Results from application to a university campus demonstrate substantial cost savings and resilience benefits compared to conventional grid-tied systems.

title: Techno-Economic Analysis of Community Wind Projects in Rhode Island
description: This study evaluates the technical and economic feasibility of community-scale wind energy projects in Rhode Island, incorporating site-specific resource assessment, interconnection costs, and community ownership financing structures. Analysis identifies favorable sites and policy mechanisms that could make community wind economically competitive with utility-scale development.

title: Grid Stability Impacts of High Renewable Penetration in Small Island Systems
description: This research analyzes frequency and voltage stability challenges associated with high penetrations of variable renewable energy in small island electricity systems using power systems simulation. We identify minimum inertia requirements and control strategies for grid-forming inverters that maintain stability at renewable penetrations exceeding 80 percent.

title: Baseline Carbon Inventory Methodology for University Campuses
description: This paper develops and validates a comprehensive greenhouse gas inventory methodology for university campuses that addresses scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions from research operations, commuting, and supply chains not captured in standard higher education protocols. The methodology is applied to establish a verified baseline for a New England research university.

title: Behavioral Interventions for Energy Conservation in Residence Halls
description: This study evaluates randomized behavioral interventions including social comparison feedback, commitment devices, and gamification on energy consumption in university residence halls. Social comparison with normative feedback produced the largest and most durable reductions in electricity and heating consumption across all treatment conditions.

title: Continuous Flow Synthesis of Pharmaceutical Intermediates Using Microreactors
description: This paper reports the development of continuous flow synthesis protocols for key pharmaceutical intermediates using microreactor technology, achieving significant improvements in yield, selectivity, and reaction safety compared to batch processes. Scale-up considerations and economic analysis are provided for three industrially relevant synthetic transformations.

title: Heterogeneous Catalysis for Selective Hydrogenation of Bio-Derived Feedstocks
description: This research develops and characterizes supported metal catalysts for the selective hydrogenation of bio-derived platform chemicals to high-value intermediates under mild process conditions. The relationship between nanoparticle size, support surface chemistry, and selectivity is elucidated through combined experimental and computational characterization.

title: Process Intensification Strategies for Solvent Recovery in API Manufacturing
description: This paper investigates process intensification strategies including membrane separation, reactive distillation, and heat integration for improving solvent recovery efficiency in active pharmaceutical ingredient manufacturing. Implementation of the integrated approach reduces solvent consumption by over 60 percent and decreases energy intensity compared to conventional distillation.

title: Valorization of Fly Ash and Slag as Supplementary Cementitious Materials
description: This study evaluates the mechanical properties, durability performance, and microstructural characteristics of concrete incorporating high-volume fly ash and ground granulated blast furnace slag as cement replacements. Optimized blends achieve equivalent or superior compressive strength and chloride resistance while significantly reducing embodied carbon.

title: Biorefinery Integration for Lignocellulosic Waste Conversion to High-Value Chemicals
description: This paper presents process design and techno-economic analysis for an integrated biorefinery converting lignocellulosic agricultural waste streams to high-value chemicals including furfural, levulinic acid, and lignin-derived aromatics. The integrated process achieves favorable economics at regional collection scales relevant to northeastern agricultural waste streams.

title: Trans-Inclusive Healthcare Policy: Gaps in State Medicaid Coverage
description: This study systematically reviews and evaluates state Medicaid coverage policies for gender-affirming healthcare services, identifying gaps in coverage for transgender and nonbinary enrollees across the United States. We document widespread exclusion of surgical care, inconsistent coverage of hormone therapy, and administrative barriers that impede access.

title: Feminist Political Economy and Care Work in the Gig Economy
description: This research applies feminist political economy frameworks to analyze the gendered dimensions of care work organization in platform-mediated gig economy labor markets. We examine how algorithmic management and contractor classification reproduce and intensify existing care work devaluation and precarity for women workers.

title: Media Representation of LGBTQ+ Identities in Prime-Time Television
description: This paper conducts a content analysis of LGBTQ+ character representation in prime-time broadcast and streaming television from 2015 to 2023, examining visibility, narrative roles, and intersectional identity dimensions. Despite increased overall representation, we find persistent deficits in portrayal of transgender, bisexual, and LGBTQ+ people of color characters.

title: Remittances Financial Inclusion and Development in Sending Communities
description: This study examines how remittance flows affect financial inclusion, local investment, and development outcomes in migrant-sending communities in Central America and the Caribbean. We find that mobile money adoption for remittance receipt significantly increases formal financial sector participation and household business investment.

title: Undocumented Immigrant Labor Rights and Wage Theft in the Service Sector
description: This paper investigates the prevalence and determinants of wage theft experienced by undocumented immigrant workers in the service sector, drawing on original survey and interview data. Findings document widespread minimum wage violations, tip theft, and off-the-clock work demands concentrated in food service, domestic work, and construction.

title: Dietary Patterns and Metabolic Syndrome Risk in College-Age Adults
description: This study examines the relationship between dietary patterns identified through cluster analysis and metabolic syndrome risk factors in a cross-sectional sample of college-age adults. A Western dietary pattern characterized by high consumption of ultra-processed foods and sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with elevated risk across multiple metabolic syndrome components.

title: Food Insecurity Screening and Intervention in Primary Care Settings
description: This paper evaluates the implementation and effectiveness of standardized food insecurity screening and community resource referral protocols in primary care clinical settings. Practices that integrate screening with warm handoffs to food assistance navigators achieve significantly higher rates of successful resource connection for food-insecure patients.

title: Ultra-Processed Food Consumption and Long-Term Cardiovascular Outcomes
description: This prospective cohort study examines the association between ultra-processed food consumption and long-term cardiovascular disease incidence and mortality in a nationally representative sample of adults. A dose-response relationship between ultra-processed food intake and incident cardiovascular events persists after adjustment for overall diet quality and socioeconomic factors.

title: Structural Racism and Preterm Birth Disparities in New England
description: This research applies an index of structural racism exposure at the census tract level to examine its contribution to persistent Black-white disparities in preterm birth rates across New England. Structural racism measures including residential segregation, economic disinvestment, and differential policing explain a substantial share of the racial gap in birth outcomes.
lab_name: Public Health Systems & Health Equity Lab

title: Medicaid Managed Care and Continuity of Coverage for Low-Income Adults
description: This study evaluates whether Medicaid managed care enrollment improves continuity of health insurance coverage and reduces gaps in care for low-income adults compared to fee-for-service Medicaid. Analysis of administrative data finds that managed care enrollment is associated with reduced coverage gaps but mixed effects on preventive care utilization.
lab_name: Public Health Systems & Health Equity Lab

title: FAIR Data Principles Adoption Among Social Science Researchers
description: This paper surveys the awareness, attitudes, and data management practices of social science researchers with respect to FAIR data principles covering findability, accessibility, interoperability, and reusability. We identify disciplinary variation in adoption rates and key institutional, technical, and normative barriers to implementation.

title: Linked Open Data Infrastructure for Institutional Repository Networks
description: This research describes the development and evaluation of a linked open data infrastructure connecting distributed institutional repository systems to support cross-institutional discovery and reuse of research outputs. The system enables semantic querying across institutional boundaries and significantly improves research output discoverability for interdisciplinary users.

title: Metadata Quality Assessment in Disciplinary Data Repositories
description: This paper presents a framework and automated tools for assessing metadata quality in disciplinary research data repositories across dimensions of completeness, accuracy, consistency, and reusability. Application to four major social science data archives reveals systematic gaps in geographic coverage and temporal metadata that impede data discovery.

title: Emulation Strategies for Long-Term Preservation of Born-Digital Art Objects
description: This study evaluates emulation-based preservation strategies for born-digital artworks, software-based installations, and interactive media across a range of legacy software environments and hardware dependencies. A decision framework for selecting among emulation, migration, and hybrid preservation approaches is developed and applied to ten case studies.

title: Format Obsolescence Risk Assessment for Government Digital Records
description: This paper presents a risk assessment methodology for evaluating format obsolescence threats to government digital record collections, incorporating format adoption curves, vendor support timelines, and migration pathway availability. Application to a state government digital archive identifies high-risk format concentrations and prioritizes migration investments.

title: Neighborhood Effects on Intergenerational Income Mobility
description: This study estimates neighborhood-level effects on intergenerational income mobility using administrative tax records linked to childhood residential location data for a cohort of children born in the 1980s. We find substantial variation in upward mobility outcomes across neighborhoods that persists after controlling for family income and other individual characteristics.

title: College Major Choice and Long-Run Earnings by Race and Gender
description: This paper examines the contribution of college major selection to racial and gender earnings gaps in the labor market using longitudinal data linking college records to earnings outcomes ten years after graduation. Major segregation accounts for a substantial share of both the gender earnings gap and race-specific earnings differences, with compounding effects at their intersection.

title: Social Networks Job Matching and Labor Market Inequality
description: This research investigates how social network composition affects job search outcomes and wage determination, with particular attention to mechanisms through which network-based job matching perpetuates inequality across racial and class lines. We find that access to bridging ties in higher-wage occupational networks is a key driver of upward labor market mobility.

title: Present Bias and Health Insurance Plan Selection
description: This paper examines how present bias affects health insurance plan selection decisions in employer-sponsored and marketplace contexts using a combination of field data and laboratory experiments. Present-biased individuals systematically select plans with lower premiums but higher out-of-pocket costs, resulting in demonstrable welfare losses during adverse health events.

title: Social Comparison Nudges for Residential Energy Reduction
description: This study evaluates a randomized controlled trial of social comparison home energy reports delivered to residential utility customers, examining treatment effect heterogeneity by housing type, consumption level, and household demographics. High-consuming households show the largest conservation responses, but effects decay without repeated feedback delivery.

title: Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy of Topological Surface States
description: This paper reports angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy measurements of topological surface state band structure in a series of topological insulator thin films with varying thickness and surface termination. We characterize the Dirac cone dispersion, surface state penetration depth, and interaction between top and bottom surface states as a function of film thickness.

title: Proximity-Induced Superconductivity in Topological Insulator Thin Films
description: This research investigates the proximity-induced superconducting state at the interface between topological insulator thin films and conventional superconductors using low-temperature tunneling spectroscopy and transport measurements. Evidence for topological surface state contributions to the induced gap and signatures consistent with Majorana edge modes are reported.

title: Strain-Tunable Electronic Properties of Two-Dimensional Van der Waals Heterostructures
description: This paper demonstrates strain-tunable electronic properties in van der Waals heterostructures assembled from two-dimensional materials using a piezoelectric substrate platform for applying in-plane biaxial strain. Continuous tuning of interlayer coupling, band alignment, and moiré superlattice periodicity is achieved across strain ranges accessible with standard piezoelectric actuators.

title: Graph Neural Networks for Molecular Property Prediction in Drug Discovery
description: This study develops and benchmarks graph neural network architectures for predicting molecular properties relevant to early-stage drug discovery, including solubility, binding affinity, and toxicity. The proposed architectures incorporating hierarchical attention and 3D geometric features achieve state-of-the-art performance on standard molecular property benchmarks.

title: Ab Initio Thermodynamics of Transition Metal Oxide Catalysts
description: This paper applies density functional theory and ab initio thermodynamics to investigate the surface phase diagrams and catalytic activity of transition metal oxide catalysts under realistic reaction conditions. The approach reveals coverage-dependent active site structures and explains experimentally observed activity trends across the transition metal series.

title: Supply Chain Disruption and Inventory Resilience in Small Manufacturers
description: This research examines inventory resilience strategies adopted by small manufacturing firms in response to supply chain disruptions, drawing on survey and case study data from a sample of New England manufacturers. We find that diversified supplier networks and modest strategic buffer stock investments provide substantially higher disruption resilience than lean inventory approaches alone.

title: Customer Lifetime Value Modeling with Recurrent Neural Networks
description: This paper develops recurrent neural network models for customer lifetime value prediction using longitudinal transaction data from a retail firm, benchmarking against traditional probabilistic models. The deep learning approach demonstrates superior predictive accuracy for customers with complex or non-stationary purchasing behavior patterns.

title: Dynamic Pricing Optimization for Perishable Goods in Retail Supply Chains
description: This study presents a reinforcement learning framework for dynamic pricing of perishable goods that jointly optimizes markdown timing and discount depth to minimize waste while maximizing revenue across retail distribution networks. Simulation results and a field experiment demonstrate significant improvements in waste reduction and revenue recovery compared to rule-based pricing.

title: Pay-for-Success Contracts and Social Outcomes Measurement in Workforce Development
description: This paper examines the design and implementation of pay-for-success financing contracts in workforce development programs, with attention to outcome measurement validity and stakeholder accountability structures. Case analysis reveals significant challenges in defining and verifying employment outcome metrics that satisfy both investor and program fidelity requirements.

title: Impact Investing and Portfolio Construction in Community Development Finance
description: This research investigates portfolio construction strategies for impact investors seeking to allocate capital across community development financial institutions while balancing financial return and social impact objectives. We develop a multi-objective optimization framework incorporating blended finance instruments and impact measurement data.

title: Procedural Narrative Generation for Adaptive Storytelling Systems
description: This paper presents a procedural narrative generation system for interactive digital media that adapts story progression to player choices using a hierarchical planning architecture combined with natural language generation. Evaluation with human participants shows significant improvements in narrative coherence and player agency perception compared to template-based systems.

title: Haptic Feedback Design in Immersive Virtual Reality Art Experiences
description: This research investigates the contribution of haptic feedback modalities to presence, embodiment, and aesthetic engagement in immersive virtual reality art installations. A series of experimental installations and controlled user studies identify interaction design principles that enhance affective response without disrupting narrative immersion.

title: Generative AI and Authorship in Contemporary Digital Art Practice
description: This paper examines how artists working with generative AI tools conceptualize authorship, creativity, and originality in their practice, drawing on in-depth interviews with professional digital artists. We develop a taxonomy of human-AI creative collaboration modes and analyze their implications for copyright, attribution, and art market valuation.

title: Settler Colonialism and the Curation of Indigenous Artifacts in U.S. Museums
description: This study critically examines curatorial practices and repatriation policies governing indigenous cultural objects in major U.S. museum collections through the lens of settler colonial theory. We analyze NAGPRA implementation records and institutional policies to assess the gap between decolonization rhetoric and actual repatriation practice.

title: Propaganda Aesthetics and the Visual Politics of Climate Communication
description: This paper examines the visual rhetoric and aesthetic strategies deployed in climate communication materials across political and advocacy contexts, analyzing how visual framing shapes public climate risk perception and policy support. We identify aestheticization of disaster imagery and greenwashing visual strategies as particularly influential in shaping audience responses.

title: Seasonal Hypoxia Dynamics and Benthic Faunal Response in Narragansett Bay
description: This study documents seasonal dissolved oxygen dynamics and benthic invertebrate community responses to hypoxic events in Narragansett Bay using high-frequency sensor data and benthic sampling. Results show that hypoxic event frequency and duration have increased over the monitoring period, driving significant shifts in benthic community composition.

title: Harmful Algal Bloom Detection Using Remote Sensing and In-Situ Sensors
description: This paper presents a multi-sensor monitoring framework for early detection and tracking of harmful algal blooms in coastal waters using satellite remote sensing combined with autonomous in-situ sensor networks. Machine learning classification applied to multi-spectral imagery achieves high detection accuracy and enables advance warning lead times of 3 to 5 days.

title: Long-Term Trends in Phytoplankton Community Composition in Rhode Island Sound
description: This research analyzes a 25-year time series of phytoplankton community composition data from Rhode Island Sound to document climate-driven changes in species assemblages and phenology. Significant shifts toward smaller cell sizes and changes in the seasonal timing of bloom events are documented and attributed to warming and stratification changes.

title: Living Shoreline Effectiveness for Erosion Control and Habitat Provision
description: This study evaluates the performance of living shoreline installations relative to traditional hardened shoreline structures for erosion control and habitat provision across multiple New England coastal sites. Living shorelines achieve comparable erosion control while providing substantially greater fish and invertebrate habitat area and water quality benefits.

title: Flood Risk Communication and Managed Retreat Acceptance in Coastal Communities
description: This paper examines how risk communication framing and community engagement processes affect acceptance of managed retreat from flood-prone coastal areas among affected residents. Participatory planning processes that center community values and long-term place attachment significantly increase willingness to consider managed retreat compared to top-down risk communication.

title: Hydrodynamic Load Analysis for Floating Offshore Wind Turbine Foundations
description: This research presents coupled hydrodynamic and structural analysis of floating offshore wind turbine foundation systems under combined wave, current, and wind loading representative of North Atlantic environmental conditions. Results characterize foundation response, mooring system fatigue, and power performance under extreme and operational sea states.

title: Structural Fatigue Assessment of Steel Catenary Risers Under Ocean Current Loading
description: This paper conducts fatigue life assessment of steel catenary risers subject to vortex-induced vibration under characteristic Gulf Stream and shelf-edge current loading conditions. A frequency-domain spectral fatigue analysis calibrated with field measurements identifies critical riser sections and informs inspection interval requirements.

title: Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Path Planning for Seafloor Mapping
description: This study develops and evaluates an adaptive path planning algorithm for autonomous underwater vehicles conducting seafloor bathymetric and habitat mapping missions under vehicle endurance and communication constraints. The algorithm optimizes coverage efficiency by dynamically reallocating waypoints based on real-time data quality assessment.

title: Capacity Value of Offshore Wind in Deregulated Electricity Markets
description: This paper estimates the capacity value of offshore wind generation in deregulated northeastern electricity markets using probabilistic methods calibrated to regional load and wind resource data. Results quantify the contribution of offshore wind to resource adequacy and inform market design rules for capacity auction eligibility.
lab_name: Offshore Renewable Energy & Grid Integration Lab

title: Transmission Planning for High Offshore Wind Penetration in the Northeast Grid
description: This research analyzes transmission infrastructure requirements and planning challenges for integrating large-scale offshore wind procurement targets into the northeastern U.S. electricity grid. Contingency analysis and production cost modeling identify critical transmission upgrade needs and the role of offshore transmission networks in reducing curtailment.
lab_name: Offshore Renewable Energy & Grid Integration Lab

title: Polygenic Risk Score Validation in Diverse Ancestry Cohorts
description: This study evaluates the transferability and predictive accuracy of polygenic risk scores developed in European ancestry cohorts when applied to individuals of African, Latin American, and East Asian ancestry. We demonstrate systematic degradation in predictive performance across ancestry groups and develop ancestry-specific recalibration approaches.

title: Single-Cell RNA Sequencing of Tumor Microenvironments in Breast Cancer
description: This paper reports single-cell RNA sequencing characterization of cellular composition and transcriptional states in the tumor microenvironment across breast cancer subtypes, identifying distinct immune cell populations associated with treatment response and prognosis. Cell-cell communication analysis reveals subtype-specific signaling networks that represent potential therapeutic targets.

title: Pharmacogenomic Markers for Chemotherapy Toxicity Prediction
description: This study identifies and validates pharmacogenomic markers predictive of severe adverse toxicity to platinum-based and taxane chemotherapy regimens in a prospective clinical cohort. A multi-gene panel outperforms individual variant associations and clinical risk factors for predicting grade 3 to 4 toxicities requiring dose modification or treatment discontinuation.

title: CRISPR-Mediated Metabolic Engineering of Yeast for Terpenoid Overproduction
description: This paper describes the design and application of a multiplex CRISPR-Cas9 metabolic engineering strategy in Saccharomyces cerevisiae to redirect carbon flux toward terpenoid biosynthesis pathways. The engineered strains achieve high-titer production of high-value sesquiterpene and diterpene target compounds under optimized fed-batch fermentation conditions.

title: Fed-Batch Bioprocess Optimization for Monoclonal Antibody Yield and Quality
description: This research develops and applies design of experiments and model-based optimization approaches to fed-batch monoclonal antibody bioprocess parameters including feeding strategy, dissolved oxygen setpoint, and pH control. The optimized process achieves significant improvements in final titer and glycosylation profile consistency relative to baseline conditions.

title: Active Matter Dynamics in Dense Bacterial Suspensions
description: This paper investigates collective motion dynamics, turbulent-like flow patterns, and topological defect formation in dense bacterial suspensions as a model active matter system using high-resolution microscopy and particle tracking. The relationship between swimming speed, cell density, and collective behavior transitions is characterized experimentally and compared with active hydrodynamic theories.

title: Mechanotransduction Pathways in Cell Migration Through Confined Geometries
description: This study examines how cells sense and respond to physical confinement during migration through microfabricated channels of controlled geometry, focusing on nuclear deformation, cytoskeletal remodeling, and mechanotransduction signaling. Results reveal nuclear stiffness as a key determinant of migration speed in tight confinement and identify LINC complex components as critical mechanosensors.

title: Quantitative Phase Imaging of Cell Volume Regulation Under Osmotic Stress
description: This paper applies quantitative phase imaging to non-invasively measure cell dry mass and volume dynamics during regulatory volume decrease responses to hypo-osmotic challenge in multiple cell types. The approach reveals cell-type-specific volume regulation kinetics and the contribution of cytoskeletal reorganization to rapid osmotic adaptation.

title: Theta Oscillations and Spatial Memory Encoding in Hippocampal Place Cells
description: This study investigates the relationship between hippocampal theta oscillation phase and spike timing in place cells during active spatial exploration and memory-guided navigation in rodents. Phase precession analysis reveals systematic relationships between theta phase coding, running speed, and spatial memory performance on a maze task.

title: Synaptic Plasticity Rules for Sequence Learning in Cortical Circuits
description: This paper develops and analyzes biophysically constrained models of spike-timing-dependent synaptic plasticity rules in cortical circuits and examines their capacity to support temporal sequence learning and replay. Simulations show that asymmetric STDP windows combined with homeostatic scaling enable reliable sequence storage and spontaneous reactivation.

title: Touch DNA Recovery Efficiency from Common Substrate Materials
description: This study systematically evaluates touch DNA recovery efficiency from common substrate materials including glass, metal, plastic, and fabric using multiple collection and extraction protocols. We identify substrate-specific protocols that maximize cellular material recovery and provide statistical models for interpreting low-template forensic touch DNA evidence.

title: Low-Copy-Number STR Profiling with Next-Generation Sequencing Platforms
description: This paper evaluates next-generation sequencing platforms for short tandem repeat profiling of low-copy-number forensic DNA samples, comparing performance against capillary electrophoresis for allele detection sensitivity and mixture interpretation accuracy. Sequencing-based STR typing demonstrates superior sensitivity for degraded samples while enabling sequence-level allele discrimination.

title: Trace Fiber Analysis and Probabilistic Source Attribution in Investigations
description: This research develops probabilistic frameworks for evaluating the strength of evidence from trace fiber comparisons in forensic investigations, incorporating population frequency databases and transfer persistence data. The Bayesian approach provides defensible likelihood ratio statistics that can be communicated transparently in expert testimony.

title: Mechanochemical Synthesis of Pharmaceuticals Without Organic Solvents
description: This paper demonstrates solvent-free mechanochemical synthesis routes for a series of pharmaceutical active ingredients and key intermediates using ball milling technology. The approach achieves comparable or superior yields and purity to solution-phase synthesis while eliminating organic solvent waste streams and reducing reaction times.

title: Bio-Catalytic Routes to Fine Chemicals Using Engineered Enzyme Cascades
description: This research develops engineered enzyme cascade reactions for the biocatalytic synthesis of chiral fine chemicals from bio-derived starting materials under mild aqueous conditions. Protein engineering strategies applied to key bottleneck enzymes improve activity, selectivity, and operational stability sufficient for preparative-scale synthesis.

title: PFAS Contamination in Southern New England Groundwater: Exposure and Health Risk
description: This study characterizes per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance contamination in private well and public groundwater supplies across southern New England, developing spatial exposure models and quantitative health risk assessments for affected communities. We identify proximity to military airfields, fire training facilities, and industrial sites as primary determinants of PFAS exceedance risk.

title: Microplastic Ingestion and Endocrine Disruption in Estuarine Fish Populations
description: This paper documents microplastic ingestion prevalence, particle characteristics, and associated endocrine-disrupting chemical burdens in estuarine fish populations from Narragansett Bay and adjacent coastal waters. Tissue biomarker analysis reveals correlations between microplastic-associated chemical exposure and endocrine disruption endpoints in juvenile fish.

title: Cumulative Risk Assessment for Air Toxics in Environmental Justice Communities
description: This research develops and applies a cumulative risk assessment framework incorporating multiple air toxic emissions sources to characterize cancer and non-cancer health risks in environmental justice communities across Rhode Island. Results identify persistent spatial disparities in air toxic burden that disproportionately affect communities of color and low-income populations.

title: Scale-Up Challenges for Cell Therapy Manufacturing: Mixing and Oxygen Delivery
description: This paper investigates mixing uniformity and oxygen delivery challenges encountered during scale-up of cell therapy manufacturing processes from laboratory to clinical production scale. Computational fluid dynamics modeling combined with experimental validation identifies bioreactor design parameters and operating conditions that maintain cell culture performance at scale.

title: Continuous Bioprocessing for mRNA Vaccine Antigen Production
description: This study develops and characterizes an integrated continuous bioprocessing platform for mRNA vaccine antigen production combining cell-free transcription with continuous chromatographic purification. The approach achieves significant improvements in volumetric productivity and product quality consistency compared to batch manufacturing processes.

title: SNAP Participation Food Security and Health Among Children in Low-Income Households
description: This paper examines the effects of SNAP participation on food security, dietary quality, and health outcomes for children in low-income households using longitudinal survey data with quasi-experimental identification of program effects. We find that SNAP participation significantly reduces food insecurity and improves diet quality for young children, with larger effects for households with the lowest pre-transfer incomes.

title: Earned Income Tax Credit and Maternal and Infant Health Outcomes
description: This research uses variation in state EITC supplement generosity to identify causal effects of the Earned Income Tax Credit on maternal and infant health outcomes including prenatal care, birth weight, and maternal mental health. Increased EITC generosity is associated with significant improvements in birth weight and reduced rates of maternal depression.

title: Eviction Housing Instability and Long-Run Earnings in Low-Income Renters
description: This study uses court eviction records linked to administrative earnings data to estimate the long-run causal effect of eviction on earnings trajectories for low-income renters. We find that eviction causes persistent earnings losses over a ten-year window through disrupted employment relationships and geographic displacement from high-opportunity neighborhoods.

title: Structural Drivers of Black-White Hypertension Disparities in the Northeastern U.S.
description: This research applies multilevel models to examine how neighborhood-level structural factors including residential segregation, built environment characteristics, and local health resource access contribute to Black-white hypertension prevalence disparities across northeastern metropolitan areas. Structural factors explain a substantial share of racial hypertension disparities independent of individual-level risk factors.

title: Community Health Worker Programs and Chronic Disease Management in Underserved Areas
description: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of community health worker-led chronic disease management programs in reducing hospitalizations and improving disease control for patients with diabetes and hypertension in medically underserved areas. Programs with structured care coordination protocols and co-location in primary care settings demonstrate the largest improvements in clinical outcomes.

title: Critical Race Theory in Higher Education Policy: Implications for Diversity Initiatives
description: This paper examines the implications of critical race theoretical frameworks for the design and evaluation of diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives in higher education institutions. We analyze how race-neutral and race-conscious institutional policies interact with structural racism mechanisms to shape equity outcomes for students of color.

title: Policing Mass Incarceration and Community Trust in Urban Neighborhoods
description: This research investigates the relationship between local policing practices, mass incarceration exposure, and community trust in law enforcement and government institutions in urban neighborhoods of color. Survey and ethnographic data show that concentrated incarceration exposure significantly erodes institutional trust and civic participation in affected communities.

title: Environmental Racism and Siting of Industrial Facilities in Communities of Color
description: This study applies quantitative spatial analysis and historical case studies to document the disproportionate siting of environmentally burdensome industrial facilities in communities of color across the northeastern United States. The pattern is traced to the interaction of racially discriminatory zoning histories, real estate market dynamics, and weaknesses in environmental justice regulatory enforcement.

title: Heritage Language Maintenance and Identity Among Second-Generation Immigrants
description: This paper examines the relationship between heritage language maintenance practices and ethnic identity formation among second-generation immigrant youth from diverse linguistic backgrounds in a northeastern U.S. city. Family language policy, co-ethnic peer networks, and school-based heritage language programs are identified as key factors supporting heritage language retention and positive bicultural identity outcomes.

title: Intercultural Competence Development Through International Service-Learning
description: This study evaluates the effects of international service-learning programs on intercultural competence development in undergraduate students using a validated pre-post assessment framework. Structured critical reflection components and reciprocal partnership models with host communities produce significantly greater intercultural learning gains than standard program models.

title: Startup Ecosystem Mapping: URI Student Ventures 2015-2024
description: This paper presents a comprehensive mapping and analysis of student-founded ventures emerging from the University of Rhode Island from 2015 to 2024, characterizing founding team composition, sector distribution, financing outcomes, and survival rates. The analysis identifies disciplinary training, prior entrepreneurial exposure, and mentorship access as key predictors of venture formation and early success.

title: Angel Investment Readiness and University Venture Support Programs
description: This research evaluates the effectiveness of university venture support programs in improving angel investment readiness and early-stage financing outcomes for student and faculty entrepreneurs. Structured pitch coaching, investor network facilitation, and due diligence preparation programs significantly increase rates of external angel investment within 18 months.

title: Social Ventures and Hybrid Business Models in Higher Education Incubators
description: This paper examines the prevalence, characteristics, and performance outcomes of social ventures and hybrid business models incubated within university entrepreneurship programs, comparing them with purely commercial ventures in the same programs. Social ventures demonstrate lower financial return but higher founder satisfaction and measurable community benefit outcomes.

title: Food Pantry Utilization and Stigma Among College Students
description: This study investigates the relationship between perceived stigma and food pantry utilization among food-insecure college students, examining how campus climate, peer norms, and program design affect help-seeking behavior. Anti-stigma messaging and anonymous access pathways significantly increase utilization rates among eligible students who previously avoided pantry services.
lab_name: Campus Food Systems & Nutrition Access Lab

title: Farm-to-Campus Procurement and Carbon Footprint Reduction in University Dining
description: This paper evaluates the food carbon footprint implications and supply chain feasibility of increasing farm-to-campus local food procurement in university dining operations, drawing on lifecycle assessment and supply chain mapping for a New England research university. Strategic procurement shifts targeting high-impact food categories achieve meaningful carbon reductions while supporting regional agricultural economies.
lab_name: Campus Food Systems & Nutrition Access Lab

title: Devised Theater and Community Organizing in Environmental Justice Campaigns
description: This research examines the use of devised theater and performance as tools for community organizing and public narrative building in environmental justice campaigns in urban communities. Case analysis shows that participatory performance processes build community capacity and increase sustained civic engagement beyond the scope of individual campaign actions.

title: Drama Therapy Outcomes for Trauma Survivors in Refugee Communities
description: This paper evaluates the therapeutic outcomes of group drama therapy interventions for trauma survivors in refugee communities using a mixed-methods design incorporating standardized trauma symptom measures and participant narrative inquiry. Significant reductions in PTSD symptoms and social isolation are documented alongside improvements in participants' sense of agency and cultural identity expression.

title: Applied Theater as Pedagogy in Correctional Education Programs
description: This study examines the implementation and educational outcomes of applied theater programs in correctional facility educational settings, drawing on ethnographic observation and participant interviews. Theater-based pedagogy produces measurable improvements in literacy and social-emotional competencies while fostering transformative reflective practice among incarcerated participants.

title: Accessibility Design Principles for Virtual Reality Applications
description: This paper develops and evaluates a set of accessibility design principles for virtual reality applications targeting users with visual, auditory, motor, and cognitive disabilities. User testing with disability community participants identifies persistent design barriers in current consumer VR systems and validates accessible design patterns that maintain immersion while improving usability.

title: Narrative Empathy in 360-Degree Documentary Journalism
description: This research investigates the mechanisms through which 360-degree documentary journalism elicits narrative empathy and perspective-taking in audiences, using psychophysiological measurement and post-exposure interviews. Presence and narrative transportation mediate the relationship between immersive format characteristics and empathic response, with implications for documentary ethics and practice.
